title: podman start, podman kube and podman-compose can't find networks
body:
### Issue Description

Hi! I'm having issues with podman networks: neither podman start nor podman play kube nor podman-compose are able to find networks (even if they create them  themselves) when starting containers. podman network exists "network" returns 0 (i.e. it exists). The kube configuration was created on this setup just before running podman kube play and it worked fine, so much so that I'm using a script to create the pod as a temporary workaround.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. run podman start container for a container created with the --net flag
Alternatively
1. run podman kube play kube.yaml
Alternatively
1. run podman-compose -f docker-compose.yaml up

### Describe the results you received

For podman start:
Error: unable to start container "HASH": unable to find network with name or ID container_id: network not found
For podman kube:
[starting container HASH: unable to find network with name or ID podman-default-kube-network: network not found]
For podman-compose:
Error: unable to start container HASH: unable to find network with name or ID container_id: network not found

### Describe the results you expected

No errors should be produced.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Alpine Linux

### Additional information

I don't encounter the issue when run under a specific user in this machine. There are no user-configuration files and all settings are default for all tested users. This is probably something wrong with my setup, but it's also likely to caused by some undocumented configuration.

comments.body: Is your homedir under `/var/lib/vikunja`? Please try `sudo mkdir /var/lib/cni`, this should fix it if I read the problem correctly.

That worked! Thanks! Can you explain why? Especially why I could run this with another user with the same configs when it also lacks this system-wide directory. Why is this directory needed if it's always empty? Why are there not logs regarding this (not even strace!)?

It is messy, if you can read golang start here: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/5e6c0647c0c9e14e2d9f9a45868bce8b21fdf7da/libpod/networking_linux.go#L213-L236

The main problem is/was that cni tries to write /var/lib/cni and of course rootless users cannot do that by default. So I need to mount an empty dir on top of it. And if /var/lib/cni does not exists we fall back to the parent dir.
Getting cni to work rootless was more of a hack. It is much simpler with netavark now but CNI is still supported so I cannot remove these mounts.
I guess I could add some `if CNI` branch there to avoid adding this mount when netavark is in use.
Also strace should show all these mount calls, did you forgot to add `-f` to strace?

Seeing that "failed to mount" message could've been helpful also. I had `-f` in strace, but I now think I didn't let it run long enough. Running `strace -f podman-compose -f docker-compose.yaml up 2>&1 | grep "mount(" | grep "/var" | grep -v /storage/overlay` I get no output for the user that runs successfully without an existing `/var/lib/cni` directory. But for the other user I get:
```
[pid 28981] mount("/tmp/podman-run-998/libpod/tmp/rootless-netns/var/lib", "/var/lib", 0xc000730909, MS_BIND, NULL <unfinished ...>
[pid 29002] mount("/tmp/podman-run-998/libpod/tmp/rootless-netns/var/lib", "/var/lib", 0xc00025b4e9, MS_BIND, NULL) = 0
[pid 29023] mount("/tmp/podman-run-998/libpod/tmp/rootless-netns/var/lib", "/var/lib", 0xc0005921c9, MS_BIND, NULL <unfinished ...>
```
It sounds like it tried to mount `/var/lib` because `cni` didn't exist, just like you said, but why only one user tried to mount, and why did it fail?

~Anyway, my problem is fixed, I appreciate the help! If you'd like to investigate this more or develop a fix, I'd be happy to help with testing or obtaining information, otherwise feel free to close the issue.~
Actually, I don't think the issue is completely solved. If I try to run the generated kube the app doesn't work properly, like the containers can't talk to each other. It only works when creating the pod manually.
I suppose that would be another issue, so it's probably best to close this.
